meta.name: Status Epilepticus — RSI for Refractory Seizure

patient.name: Daniel Ortiz
patient.chiefComplaint: Prolonged seizure activity, not responding to bystander benzodiazepines

history.hpi: 34-year-old male with known epilepsy seizing continuously for over 20 minutes per family. Wife administered two doses of rectal diazepam from his home supply with no effect. Generalized tonic-clonic activity has never resolved. No preceding trauma, fever, or illness. Known recent non-compliance with levetiracetam due to prescription lapse.
history.lastOralIntake: Breakfast approximately 3 hours prior to seizure onset
history.events: Wife witnessed seizure onset in the kitchen. After 5 minutes she gave rectal diazepam 20 mg. Seizure continued. She gave a second dose 10 minutes later. Seizure has now been continuous for over 20 minutes. She called 911 when her husband became cyanotic.

scene.dispatch: Medic 12, respond code 3 to 1842 Linden Street, apartment 4B, for a 34-year-old male with seizure activity. Caller reports patient is still seizing.
scene.location: Second-floor apartment, small kitchen. Patient is on the tile floor between the stove and the kitchen table. Tight space, will require moving patient to the living room to work effectively.
scene.safety: Scene is safe. Clear a working area by moving chairs and the kitchen table. Padding needed for the patient against hard floor.
scene.bystanders: Wife is present, tearful but cooperative. She provides detailed medication history and confirms the timeline of bystander diazepam administration.
visualCues: Patient prone on the kitchen floor with generalized tonic-clonic activity
Clenched jaw with blood-tinged saliva at the mouth
Empty rectal diazepam applicators visible on the counter
Pill organizer with several missed doses of levetiracetam

phases.name: Arrival — Active Status Epilepticus
phases.description: Crew arrives to find patient in generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Clenched jaw prevents OPA placement. SpO2 in the low 80s despite apparent air movement with seizure activity. Bystander benzodiazepines have failed. Vascular access not yet established.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Clenched jaw with trismus, blood-tinged saliva and secretions pooling at the lips. OPA cannot be placed. NPA indicated.
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Irregular, grunting respirations associated with tonic-clonic activity. No effective tidal volume. Accessory muscle use absent due to generalized rigidity.
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong, fast radial pulse. Skin flushed and diaphoretic.

skin.color: cyanotic lips and nail beds, flushed trunk

clinicalPresentation.pupils: Pupils 5 mm bilaterally, reactive, with roving eye movements
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Generalized tonic-clonic activity involving all four extremities, ongoing
otherFindings: Small laceration to the tongue with active bleeding

expectedActions.action: Scene safety, BSI, move patient to open area and pad from hard surfaces to prevent secondary injury
Place a nasopharyngeal airway and initiate suctioning of oral secretions; OPA contraindicated due to trismus
Begin 2-person BVM with PEEP valve at 5 cmH2O and high-flow oxygen to preoxygenate and correct hypoxemia; target SpO2 94-98%
Administer Midazolam 0.2 mg/kg IM (17 mg for 85 kg patient, max 10 mg) into the lateral thigh given absence of vascular access
expectedActions.rationale: IM midazolam is the preferred route when no IV is established; shown to be at least as effective as IV lorazepam for prehospital seizure termination.

expectedActions.action: Obtain finger-stick blood glucose to rule out hypoglycemia as seizure cause
Apply cardiac monitor, SpO2, waveform capnography

phases.name: Refractory Despite IM Benzodiazepine
phases.description: Several minutes after IM midazolam, seizure activity continues unchanged. Patient remains hypoxic despite BVM attempts which are difficult due to trismus and ongoing tonic-clonic activity. Crew recognizes this is refractory status epilepticus requiring definitive airway management with RSI.
phases.triggerCondition: Seizure activity persists 5+ minutes after IM benzodiazepine with ongoing hypoxemia

transitions.condition: RSI performed successfully with confirmed tube placement
RSI delayed greater than 5 minutes with ongoing hypoxemia

clinicalPresentation.airway: Trismus persists. BVM seal difficult. Oropharyngeal secretions recurring despite suctioning.
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Ineffective spontaneous respirations with ongoing clonic activity. BVM partially effective.
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Generalized tonic-clonic activity unchanged from arrival
otherFindings: Waveform capnography shows shark-fin morphology and rising EtCO2 (respiratory acidosis from prolonged seizure)
Core temperature climbing from muscle activity

expectedActions.action: Establish IV or IO access during preoxygenation (18g or larger)
Verbalize difficult airway plan: trismus will resolve with paralytic, maintain positioning, have SGA and surgical airway kit immediately available as backup
Continue aggressive preoxygenation with 2-person BVM + PEEP 5 cmH2O + apneic oxygenation via nasal cannula at 15 LPM
Consider Fentanyl 1 mcg/kg (85 mcg) IV push 2-3 minutes prior to intubation as pre-medication to blunt sympathetic response
Administer Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV push (130 mg for 85 kg patient) as induction agent; ketamine preferred over etomidate given its anticonvulsant properties and hemodynamic stability
expectedActions.rationale: Ketamine provides sedation and is a functional anticonvulsant; avoids the adrenal suppression concern of etomidate in this septic-physiology picture.

expectedActions.action: Administer Rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV push (85 mg for 85 kg patient) as paralytic agent
Perform endotracheal intubation with video laryngoscopy; 8.0 mm cuffed ETT; limit attempt to 15 seconds or desaturation below 90%
Confirm placement with waveform capnography, bilateral breath sounds, absent epigastric sounds; secure tube and note depth at teeth

phases.name: Post-Intubation Management
phases.description: Tube placement confirmed. Paralytic has masked visible seizure activity but underlying electrical seizure may continue. Crew must provide ongoing sedation, post-intubation ventilation targeting normocapnia, and transport.
phases.triggerCondition: Successful endotracheal intubation with confirmed placement

clinicalPresentation.airway: ETT in place at 23 cm at the teeth, cuff inflated, secured
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Ventilated via BVM with PEEP 5 cmH2O at 10 breaths/min, 6-8 mL/kg tidal volume, symmetric chest rise
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Flaccid from neuromuscular blockade; visible seizure activity abolished (EEG status unknown)
otherFindings: Waveform capnography normalizing — EtCO2 trending from 64 toward 40 mmHg with controlled ventilation
SpO2 rising to 98% on ventilator FiO2 100%

expectedActions.action: Initiate post-intubation sedation: Midazolam 0.02 mg/kg (1.7 mg) IV AND Fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg (85-170 mcg) IV; titrate to RASS -3 to -4
expectedActions.rationale: Paralytic abolishes visible seizure activity but does not treat the underlying electrical seizure. Ongoing benzodiazepine sedation is critical.

expectedActions.action: Ventilate at 10 breaths/min, 6-8 mL/kg ideal body weight, titrated to EtCO2 35-45 mmHg; avoid hyperventilation
Place orogastric tube for gastric decompression
Initiate passive cooling measures for hyperthermia (38.6°C)
Transport emergently to closest appropriate ED with neurology and critical care capability; notify early
Continuous reassessment of tube placement with waveform capnography after every patient movement

phases.name: Branch: Hypoxic Cardiac Arrest from Delayed RSI
phases.description: Prolonged preoxygenation without decisive action to secure the airway, combined with ineffective BVM and ongoing metabolic demand from status epilepticus, leads to progressive hypoxia and bradycardic arrest. This branch illustrates the consequence of analysis paralysis in a patient who cannot protect their airway.
phases.triggerCondition: Failure to proceed with RSI within 5 minutes of identifying refractory status

clinicalPresentation.airway: Now flaccid with massive secretions; trismus has relaxed
otherFindings: Clear transition from tachycardia to bradycardia to PEA

expectedActions.action: Initiate high-quality CPR; treat as hypoxic PEA arrest
Definitive airway during CPR — intubate without paralytic given flaccid state, confirm with capnography
Administer Epinephrine 1 mg (1:10,000) IV/IO every 3-5 minutes
Address reversible causes: Hypoxia is the primary driver — optimize ventilation and oxygenation

criticalActions: Recognize refractory status epilepticus after failed bystander benzodiazepines
Place NPA and initiate 2-person BVM with PEEP to correct hypoxemia
Administer Midazolam 0.2 mg/kg IM (max 10 mg) given absence of IV access
Establish IV/IO access and proceed with RSI using Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg + Rocuronium 1 mg/kg
Confirm endotracheal tube placement with waveform capnography
Provide post-intubation sedation with Midazolam 0.02 mg/kg + Fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg
expectedActions: Verbalize difficult airway plan (trismus, secretions, aspiration risk)
Apneic oxygenation via NC at 15 LPM during RSI
Fentanyl 1 mcg/kg pre-medication to blunt sympathetic response
bonusActions: Articulate rationale for ketamine over etomidate in status epilepticus
Discuss that paralysis masks but does not treat ongoing electrical seizure

learningObjectives: Recognize refractory status epilepticus as an indication for RSI
Apply MATC Seizures protocol dosing for bystander and prehospital benzodiazepines
Execute RSI per MATC Airway Management protocol including induction and paralytic selection
Understand post-intubation sedation requirements in a paralyzed seizure patient
Avoid peri-intubation hypoxia through aggressive preoxygenation and apneic oxygenation
discussionQuestions: What is the rationale for IM midazolam over IV diazepam as first-line in a seizing patient without IV access?
Why is ketamine considered a reasonable induction choice in status epilepticus compared to etomidate?
How does the paralytic change your ability to monitor seizure activity, and what does that mean for ongoing sedation?
What would you do if the first intubation attempt fails and the patient desaturates below 90%?
How would your approach change if this patient were pregnant? Pediatric?
commonPitfalls: Failing to dose IM midazolam correctly and underdosing the patient
Inadequate preoxygenation before RSI, leading to peri-intubation hypoxia
Forgetting post-intubation sedation because the patient 'looks' calm from paralysis
Hyperventilating the intubated patient and causing cerebral vasoconstriction
Delaying RSI while cycling through additional benzodiazepines in a patient who cannot protect their airway
keyTakeaways: Status epilepticus is a time-critical airway emergency when benzodiazepines fail
Trismus resolves with paralytic — this is a known indication for RSI, not a contraindication
Paralysis masks visible seizure activity but the brain may still be seizing; sedate adequately
Hypoxia is the most common cause of peri-intubation arrest — preoxygenate aggressively
